rulings.Case Title: IN RE: ROBERT A. NAIFY LIVING TRUST DATED FEBRUARY 8, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Second Cause Of Action Of Fifth Amended Petition For Relief For Breach Of Fiduciary Duty; Third-Party Liability For Breach Of Trust; And Return Of Property; Action On Rejected Creditors Claim
rulings.Rulings: (Part 1 of 2) As discussed in Cortese v. Sherwood (2018) 26 Cal.App.5th 445, 454, application of Civil Code § 1714.10 requires the Court to first determine whether there is a civil conspiracy alleged that falls under subsection (a), and then to determine whether the pleaded claims fall under either of the exceptions listed in subsection (c). The elements of a civil conspiracy cause of action are the formation and operation of the conspiracy and damage to petitioner resulting from the act or acts done in furtherance of the conspiracy (Doctor's Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1989) 49 Cal.3d 39, 44). Petitioner ("Acela") alleges that John Sherwood ("John") and Robert Naify ("Robert") formed and operated a conspiracy to underfund the Francesca Naify Living Trust and to further conceal that alleged underfunding when negotiating the termination and assignment of the Trust, damaging her as a result. Although the Amended Petition alleges that John received an independent benefit from his participation in the alleged conspiracy, the Amended Petition does not allege sufficient facts to show that John received benefits other than what he earned as fees for his legal services. Petitioner has conceded that she "will not contest that issue here" (Responsive Brief in Opposition, 12:20-22). Thus, the exception under § 1714.10(c)(2) is not applicable here. As discussed in the case law (e.g. Pavicich v. Santucci (2000) 85 Cal.App.4th 382, 395), the legislative history and wording of Civil Code § 1714.10(a) excludes from its scope an essential element of a conspiracy cause of action: that the attorney accused of conspiracy owed an independent duty to the plaintiff. This makes any viable civil conspiracy cause of action exempt from § 1714.10(a)'s procedural hurdles if the petitioner can allege that the accused attorney owned him or her an independent duty. A civil conspiracy action cannot be brought against an alleged conspirator who was not "personally bound by the duty violated by the wrongdoing" and who was acting only as the agent of the person who did have that duty. (Doctor's Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1989) 49 Cal.3d 39, 44).

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: ROBERT A. NAIFY LIVING TRUST DATED FEBRUARY 8, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant John Sherwoods (In His Individual Capacity) Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Acela Corteses Fifth Amended Petition
rulings.Rulings: Defendant John Sherwood's (in his individual capacity) Motion to Strike Fifth Amended Petition is denied as moot, as Petitioner will not be given leave to file second cause of action for civil conspiracy under Civil Code § 1714.10. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Tuesday, Janaury 29, 2019.

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: ROBERT A. NAIFY LIVING TRUST DATED FEBRUARY 8, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Leave To File Second Cause Of Action Of Fifth Amended Petition For Relief For Breach Of Fiduciary Duty; Third-Party Liability For Breach Of Trust; And Return Of Property; Action On Rejected Creditors Claim (Added For Posting Of Tentative Only)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 2) Acela alleges that John was her attorney prior to the discussions concerning the alleged underfunding of the Francesca Naify Living Trust, and the negotiated termination of that Trust, that owed her an independent duty in the course of those discussions and negotiations. The letters submitted in support of Acela's Responsive Brief in Opposition indicate that John represented Acela in 1987. Acela alleges that John obtained confidential information about her financial state and financial naiveté through this representation, and used that knowledge to Robert's advantage in false representations to Acela's attorney Glenn P. Olean and herself in 1999 regarding the Trust's funding, and also in Robert's negotiations with Acela and her sister in 2009, in connection with the termination of the Trust. Per Oasis West Realty v. Goldman (2011) 51 Cal.4th 811, 821, an "attorney may not do anything that will injuriously affect the former client in any manner in which the attorney formerly represented the client nor may the attorney at any time use against the former client knowledge or information acquired by virtue of the previous relationship." However, the Amended Petition alleges that both John and Robert knew of Acela's addiction and incarceration and her impecunious and vulnerable state during the negotiation - indeed, her continuing need for funds is allegedly what prompted the 2009 negotiation. The Amended Petition further alleges that Robert knew at the time of the negotiations that Acela had children to support and had limited employment opportunities. There is no allegation that Robert learned this only from John; rather the inference from the allegations is that Robert knew of Acela's dire circumstances independent of John's knowledge or prior representation of Acela against her creditors. Moreover, the nexus between John's alleged representation in 1987 and the alleged conspiracy to breach Robert's duty to the Trust's beneficiaries ten and twenty years later is too attenuated for the Court to infer from the allegations that John used confidential information obtained from Acela to breach a duty owed to her in his representation of Robert. In short, the Amended Petition's allegations are insufficient to infer that John violated a duty owed to Acela as her former attorney when he acted as Robert's agent when Robert allegedly underfunded and undervalued the Trust, and later negotiated the Assignment Agreement. The Motion for Leave to File Second Cause of Action of Fifth Amended Petition is denied. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Tuesday, January 29, 2019.
